full_response:
To perform matrix multiplication of two square matrices in a general form, we need to compute the product matrix \([M][N]\), where both \([M]\) and \([N]\) are \(3 \times 3\) matrices.

Firstly, let's denote:
\[ 
[M] = \begin{bmatrix} M_{11} & M_{12} & M_{13} \\ M_{21} & M_{22} & M_{23} \\ M_{31} & M_{32} & M_{33} \end{bmatrix} 
\]
and
\[
[N] = \begin{bmatrix} N_{11} & N_{12} & N_{13} \\ N_{21} & N_{22} & N_{23} \\ N_{31} & N_{32} & N_{33} \end{bmatrix} 
\]

The resulting matrix \([MN]\) will also be a \(3 \times 3\) matrix and its entries \((MN)_{ij}\) will be computed according to the matrix product rule. Each entry \((MN)_{ij}\) of the product matrix is obtained by:
\[
(MN)_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{ik} N_{kj}
\]

This sum notation states that for each element of the product matrix \([MN]\), we take the dot product of the \(i\)-th row of the matrix \([M]\) with the \(j\)-th column of the matrix \([N]\).

Thus, the complete resulting matrix \([MN]\) is given by:
\[
[M][N] = \begin{bmatrix}
\sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{1k} N_{k1} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{1k} N_{k2} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{1k} N_{k3} \\
\sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{2k} N_{k1} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{2k} N_{k2} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{2k} N_{k3} \\
\sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{3k} N_{k1} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{3k} N_{k2} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{3k} N_{k3}
\end{bmatrix}
\]

This notation compactly expresses how entries of the product matrix are formed via the summation over the multiplying indices.

\[
\boxed{\begin{bmatrix}
\sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{1k} N_{k1} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{1k} N_{k2} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{1k} N_{k3} \\
\sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{2k} N_{k1} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{2k} N_{k2} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{2k} N_{k3} \\
\sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{3k} N_{k1} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{3k} N_{k2} & \sum_{k=1}^{3} M_{3k} N_{k3}
\end{bmatrix}}
\]

Confidence: 100%
